content.text: What Scaling Laws Are
Scaling laws are empirical power-law relationships that describe how the
test loss of a neural language model
changes as a function of three quantities: the number of model parameters
, the number of training tokens
, and the total compute budget

measured in floating-point operations (FLOPs). First systematically characterized by Kaplan et al. at OpenAI in 2020 ("Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models") and later refined by Hoffmann et al. at DeepMind in 2022 (the "Chinchilla" paper), these relationships follow smooth power laws across many orders of magnitude — from million-parameter models trained on megabytes of text to hundred-billion-parameter models trained on trillions of tokens.
The core insight is that model loss
decreases predictably as each of
, and

increase, and these dependencies are approximately separable — meaning you can study each variable in isolation. In a log-log plot, the relationship is a straight line with a negative slope, which corresponds to a power law:
for some exponent
that empirically falls in the range 0.05–0.35 depending on which variable

you are varying. This means that doubling your compute budget does not double your model quality, but improvement is nonetheless reliable, smooth, and — crucially — predictable before you commit to a full training run.
The landscape of scaling laws has two landmark eras. In the Kaplan era (2020–2022), the dominant belief was that parameters
should be scaled much faster than data
, leading to the design of GPT-3 (175B parameters, 300B tokens). In the Chinchilla era (2022–present), the correct balance was shown to be equal scaling of both
and

— with the rule of thumb that you should train on roughly 20 tokens per parameter. Chinchilla (70B parameters, 1.4T tokens) was trained on the same compute as Gopher (280B parameters, 300B tokens) and outperformed it on virtually every benchmark.
Why Scaling Laws Matter

Training a large language model is one of the most expensive engineering undertakings in modern AI. A single training run for a frontier model can cost tens of millions of dollars in compute. Before scaling laws were understood, practitioners had no rigorous way to answer the most important design question:
given a fixed compute budget, how should you allocate it between model size and training data?
The answer was often guesswork informed by rule-of-thumb, leading to systematically suboptimal models.
Scaling laws solve this by enabling
compute-optimal training
: given any budget
, they tell you the optimal model size
and optimal token count

that will minimize final loss. This is not merely a theoretical refinement — the Chinchilla paper demonstrated that virtually every large model released between 2020 and 2022 (GPT-3, Gopher, Megatron-Turing NLG) was significantly undertrained: they had been allocated too many parameters and too few training tokens, resulting in higher loss than could have been achieved with the same compute. By applying the Chinchilla scaling law, future models could achieve the same or better performance at a fraction of the parameter count, dramatically reducing inference costs.
Beyond training efficiency, scaling laws matter for
research planning

. A team can run a series of small experiments, fit a power law to the results, and then extrapolate to predict what performance a much larger model would achieve — often accurately — without running the full expensive training. This is the basis of how companies like OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Anthropic make architectural decisions before committing to frontier-scale compute.
Benefits
Predictable returns:
Loss improvements follow smooth, well-characterized power laws across many orders of magnitude in scale, making outcomes highly predictable given measured scaling coefficients.
Compute budget optimization:
Given any fixed FLOPs budget, the Chinchilla law specifies the unique model size and token count that minimizes final loss — eliminating expensive guesswork about resource allocation.
Architecture comparison:

By running isoFLOP comparisons (training multiple architectures at the same compute budget), teams can identify which design choices actually improve the scaling coefficient and which are irrelevant at small scale but meaningful at large scale.
Early experiment extrapolation:

A power law fit on small-scale experiments (millions of parameters) can accurately predict performance at billion-parameter scale, enabling low-cost validation of research directions before committing to large training runs.
Inference cost forecasting:
Because the Chinchilla law tends to produce smaller, better-trained models, applying it reduces not just training cost but ongoing inference cost — a critical factor for deployed products.
Motivating continued investment:

Smooth, predictable scaling provides a roadmap: as long as the power law holds and more compute becomes available, better models will follow. This has been a major factor driving the pace of LLM development since 2020.
Trade-offs and Limitations
Slow improvement per doubling:
Power laws with exponents below 0.3 mean you need to increase compute by roughly 10× to get a 2× improvement in loss. The returns are real but require massive investment.
Emergent abilities break the smooth curve:

Certain capabilities (multi-step arithmetic, chain-of-thought reasoning, in-context learning) appear discontinuously at particular scales rather than smoothly improving. Scaling laws fitted on cross-entropy loss do not predict these phase transitions.
Architecture-dependent exponents:
The scaling coefficients
α
and
β
differ between transformer variants, mixture-of-experts models, and state-space models. A power law fitted on one architecture family does not transfer cleanly to another.
Data quality is not captured:

The standard Chinchilla law treats all tokens as equivalent. In practice, high-quality curated data is worth far more than equivalent amounts of noisy web text. Poor data quality can bend the loss curve significantly above the theoretical minimum.
Evaluation metric mismatch:

Scaling laws are fitted to cross-entropy loss on the pretraining distribution. Performance on downstream benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval) often improves non-linearly and unpredictably with respect to pretraining loss, limiting direct applicability.
Compute definition ambiguity:
The approximation
assumes dense transformer training. Models with sparse activations (MoE), grouped-query attention, or other efficiency techniques change the constant factor significantly, requiring recalibration.
Domain and modality specificity:
Most published scaling laws are for language models on English text. Scaling laws for code, images, audio, and scientific domains have different exponents and sometimes different functional forms.
How Scaling Laws Work: Core Mechanics
The Kaplan et al. (2020) Formulation
The original OpenAI scaling paper proposed that loss depends on parameters
and data tokens
with an additive structure, where each variable contributes independently:
Here
and
are critical scale constants fitted to data,
is the irreducible loss (the entropy of the true data distribution), and the exponents
and
were reported in the original paper. The key implication of this formulation: when compute-optimal, parameters should scale much faster than data (
), which is why GPT-3 was designed the way it was.
The Hoffmann et al. (2022) Chinchilla Formulation
Hoffmann et al. proposed a modified loss function that fits empirical data better over a wider compute range. They introduced a different parameterization:
where
is the estimated entropy of natural language (irreducible loss),
, and
. These are fitted to hundreds of training runs spanning model sizes from 70M to 16B parameters and token counts from 5B to 500B. The critical derivation: given a fixed compute budget
(for dense transformer training), minimize
subject to the constraint, yielding:

attrs.latex: N_{\text{opt}} = G \cdot C^{a}, \quad D_{\text{opt}} = G^{-1} \cdot C^{b}

, and
is a constant determined by the fitted coefficients. In the Chinchilla paper,
— meaning parameters and tokens should scale roughly equally with compute, and the practical rule of thumb is
The Compute Approximation
The relationship between FLOPs
, parameters
, and tokens
for a standard dense transformer is:

The factor of 6 accounts for 2 multiply-adds per forward pass (through the attention and MLP blocks), times 3 for the backward pass (gradient of output, gradient of weights). This approximation holds to within 10–20% for most transformer architectures, and is accurate enough for scaling law purposes. For an actual
-layer transformer with hidden dimension
, the exact count differs because of attention operations (
per layer for sequence length
), but the dominant term at practical model sizes is the MLP, which gives the
approximation.
Mental Model: The Bakery Analogy
Imagine you are running a bakery and you have a fixed budget of 1,000 hours of oven time this year. You have two choices about how to use that time:
hire more bakers (parameters)
or
source more recipe books (data)

. The Kaplan era said: hire more bakers, even if they have few recipes — a larger team can innovate. The Chinchilla era said: that's wrong. Each baker needs enough recipes to reach their potential, and a smaller team with a rich recipe library will outperform a large team with sparse knowledge. The sweet spot is balanced: approximately one recipe book per baker-hour. This is the 20-tokens-per-parameter rule.
A related way to build intuition: think of scaling laws as
isoFLOP curves
. For a fixed compute budget, you can trace all the (N, D) pairs that exhaust exactly that budget (
). Each point on this curve gives a different trained model. The loss varies along the curve, forming a U-shape (or more precisely, a curve with a minimum) in

space. The minimum of that curve is the compute-optimal model. Moving left (fewer parameters, more data) or right (more parameters, fewer data) from that minimum results in higher loss for the same compute cost.
Architecture and Flow

The diagram below shows three views of neural scaling laws: the log-log power law relationship between loss and scale, the key difference between the Kaplan and Chinchilla recommendations, and the compute allocation flow from budget to optimal parameters and tokens.

attrs.alt: Neural Scaling Laws diagram showing power law loss curves, Kaplan vs Chinchilla comparison, and compute-optimal allocation flow

content.text: In the left panel, all three scaling axes (parameters
, data
, compute

) show the same qualitative power-law behavior in log-log space: a straight line with negative slope. The middle panel shows the critical difference between Kaplan's recommendation (scale parameters heavily, data lightly) and Chinchilla's finding (scale both equally). The right panel shows the computational graph: given a budget
, the Chinchilla formula splits it equally between model capacity and training tokens, yielding an optimal loss that scales as
When to Use Scaling Laws
Appropriate Scenarios
Planning a large training run:

Before committing GPU-hours to a multi-billion-parameter model, run pilot experiments at 1M–100M parameters, fit a power law, and extrapolate to predict final loss and the optimal parameter/token split at your target compute budget.
Architecture search:
Run isoFLOP sweeps (multiple architectures, same compute budget) to identify which architectural changes improve the scaling coefficient
α
rather than just adding parameters.
Making the compute vs. model-size tradeoff:
When you need to decide between training a large model for fewer steps versus a smaller model for more steps, use the Chinchilla law to find the compute-optimal point.
Predicting inference cost:
Use scaling laws to find the smallest model that achieves a target loss — this model minimizes serving cost for a given quality level.
Comparing data mixtures:
Hold model size and compute fixed while varying data sources to measure which mixture produces the best-fitting scaling law (lower loss at the same scale).
When Scaling Laws Do Not Apply
Evaluating specific downstream capabilities:

Cross-entropy loss scaling laws do not reliably predict when a model will first exhibit emergent capabilities like multi-step reasoning, tool use, or instruction following. Benchmark performance can jump discontinuously.
Fine-tuning and RLHF:
Scaling laws are characterized for pretraining from scratch. Instruction tuning, RLHF, and DPO regimes have different dynamics that are not well captured by the standard formulation.
Non-transformer architectures:

Published exponents are calibrated for standard dense transformer decoders. Applying them to SSMs (Mamba), MoE models, or architectures with significantly different compute-per-parameter ratios requires new empirical calibration.
Short context or domain-specific tasks:
When pretraining on a narrow domain (protein sequences, code in a single language), the irreducible loss term
and fitted coefficients will differ substantially from general language model scaling laws.
How to Implement Scaling Law Analysis

The workflow for applying scaling laws has three phases: run a grid of small training experiments, fit a power law to the results, and extrapolate to predict large-scale behavior. The code below shows a complete implementation of each phase.
Step 1: Fitting the Chinchilla Loss Model

# scaling_laws.py — fit and predict the Chinchilla loss model
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit, minimize
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class ScalingLawParams:
    """Fitted parameters for the Chinchilla loss model."""
    E: float      # irreducible loss (natural language entropy)
    A: float      # parameter scaling coefficient
    alpha: float  # parameter scaling exponent
    B: float      # data scaling coefficient
    beta: float   # data scaling exponent

# Chinchilla published constants (Hoffmann et al. 2022)
CHINCHILLA_PARAMS = ScalingLawParams(
    E=1.69, A=406.4, alpha=0.34, B=410.7, beta=0.28
)

def chinchilla_loss(N: float, D: float, params: ScalingLawParams) -> float:
    """
    Predict cross-entropy loss given model size N (params) and
    training tokens D, using the Chinchilla scaling law.
    """
    return params.E + params.A / (N ** params.alpha) + params.B / (D ** params.beta)

def fit_scaling_law(
    N_values: np.ndarray,
    D_values: np.ndarray,
    loss_values: np.ndarray,
) -> ScalingLawParams:
    """
    Fit the Chinchilla loss model to observed (N, D, loss) triples
    using nonlinear least squares.
    """
    def model(ND, E, A, alpha, B, beta):
        N, D = ND
        return E + A / (N ** alpha) + B / (D ** beta)

    p0 = [1.7, 400.0, 0.34, 400.0, 0.28]
    bounds = ([0, 0, 0.01, 0, 0.01], [5, 1e6, 1.0, 1e6, 1.0])

    popt, _ = curve_fit(
        model,
        (N_values, D_values),
        loss_values,
        p0=p0,
        bounds=bounds,
        maxfev=10000,
    )
    return ScalingLawParams(*popt)

def compute_optimal_allocation(
    C: float,
    params: ScalingLawParams,
) -> tuple[float, float, float]:
    """
    Given compute budget C (in FLOPs), find optimal N and D
    that minimize loss subject to C = 6 * N * D.

    Returns: (N_opt, D_opt, predicted_loss)
    """
    def loss_given_N(log_N):
        N = np.exp(log_N)
        D = C / (6.0 * N)  # constraint: C = 6ND
        return chinchilla_loss(N, D, params)

    # Search log-space of N from 1M to 1T parameters
    result = minimize(
        loss_given_N,
        x0=np.log(1e8),  # start at 100M
        bounds=[(np.log(1e6), np.log(1e12))],
        method="L-BFGS-B",
    )
    N_opt = np.exp(result.x[0])
    D_opt = C / (6.0 * N_opt)
    L_opt = chinchilla_loss(N_opt, D_opt, params)
    return N_opt, D_opt, L_opt


# Example usage: plan a training run with 1e22 FLOPs
if __name__ == "__main__":
    C = 1e22  # ~10^22 FLOPs (GPT-3 class compute)
    N_opt, D_opt, L_opt = compute_optimal_allocation(C, CHINCHILLA_PARAMS)

    print(f"Compute budget:     {C:.2e} FLOPs")
    print(f"Optimal parameters: {N_opt/1e9:.1f}B")
    print(f"Optimal tokens:     {D_opt/1e9:.0f}B")
    print(f"Tokens per param:   {D_opt/N_opt:.1f}")
    print(f"Predicted loss:     {L_opt:.4f}")
Code Examples
Example: Fitting Power Laws to Your Own Training Data

# power_law_fit.py — fit a power law to observed loss curves
# from your own small-scale training runs
import numpy as np
from scipy.stats import linregress
import json

def fit_power_law_single_variable(
    scale_values: np.ndarray,
    loss_values: np.ndarray,
    irreducible_loss: float = 1.69,
) -> tuple[float, float, float]:
    """
    Fit L(X) = L_inf + A * X^(-alpha) by linearizing:
    log(L - L_inf) = log(A) - alpha * log(X)

    Returns (A, alpha, r_squared).
    """
    excess_loss = loss_values - irreducible_loss
    # Remove any non-positive values (can't log them)
    mask = excess_loss > 0
    log_x = np.log(scale_values[mask])
    log_y = np.log(excess_loss[mask])

    slope, intercept, r_value, _, _ = linregress(log_x, log_y)
    alpha = -slope
    A = np.exp(intercept)
    return A, alpha, r_value**2

def predict_from_power_law(X: float, A: float, alpha: float, L_inf: float = 1.69) -> float:
    return L_inf + A * X**(-alpha)

# Simulate observations from a hypothetical sweep over N
# In practice, these come from actual training runs
np.random.seed(42)
N_observed = np.array([1e7, 3e7, 1e8, 3e8, 1e9, 3e9])
true_A, true_alpha = 406.4, 0.34
D_fixed = 20 * N_observed  # 20 tokens per param
true_loss = 1.69 + true_A / N_observed**true_alpha + 410.7 / D_fixed**0.28
observed_loss = true_loss + np.random.normal(0, 0.005, len(N_observed))

A_fit, alpha_fit, r2 = fit_power_law_single_variable(N_observed, observed_loss)
print(f"Fitted:  A = {A_fit:.1f}  alpha = {alpha_fit:.4f}  R^2 = {r2:.4f}")
print(f"True:    A = {true_A:.1f}  alpha = {true_alpha:.4f}")

# Extrapolate to 70B and 175B
for N_target in [7e10, 1.75e11]:
    D_target = 20 * N_target
    L_pred = predict_from_power_law(N_target, A_fit, alpha_fit)
    print(f"Predicted loss at N={N_target/1e9:.0f}B: {L_pred:.4f}")
Real-World Example: The Chinchilla Training Decision

The most influential application of scaling laws in practice was the design of Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al., 2022). The DeepMind team had already trained Gopher, a 280B parameter model, on 300B tokens using a compute budget of approximately
FLOPs. The question was: was this compute well spent? Applying the scaling law to this budget reveals the answer starkly.

# Reproducing the Chinchilla planning calculation for Gopher vs. Chinchilla
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import minimize

E, A, alpha_n, B, beta = 1.69, 406.4, 0.34, 410.7, 0.28

def loss(N, D):
    return E + A / N**alpha_n + B / D**beta

def find_optimal(C):
    def objective(log_N):
        N = np.exp(log_N)
        D = C / (6 * N)
        return loss(N, D)
    res = minimize(objective, x0=np.log(1e9), bounds=[(np.log(1e6), np.log(1e12))], method="L-BFGS-B")
    N = np.exp(res.x[0])
    D = C / (6 * N)
    return N, D

# Gopher's actual compute budget
C_gopher = 6 * 280e9 * 300e9  # = 5.04e23 FLOPs
# Chinchilla used roughly the same compute
C_chinchilla = 6 * 70e9 * 1400e9  # = 5.88e23 FLOPs

print("=== Gopher (DeepMind, 2021) ===")
N_gopher, D_gopher = 280e9, 300e9
print(f"  Params: {N_gopher/1e9:.0f}B  Tokens: {D_gopher/1e9:.0f}B")
print(f"  Compute: {C_gopher:.2e} FLOPs")
print(f"  Loss: {loss(N_gopher, D_gopher):.4f}")
print(f"  Tokens/param: {D_gopher/N_gopher:.1f}  (optimal would be ~20)")

print()
print("=== What the scaling law says was optimal for Gopher's budget ===")
N_opt, D_opt = find_optimal(C_gopher)
print(f"  N_opt: {N_opt/1e9:.1f}B  D_opt: {D_opt/1e9:.0f}B")
print(f"  Tokens/param: {D_opt/N_opt:.1f}")
print(f"  Optimal loss: {loss(N_opt, D_opt):.4f}")
print(f"  Gopher loss gap: {loss(N_gopher, D_gopher) - loss(N_opt, D_opt):.4f}")

print()
print("=== Chinchilla (DeepMind, 2022) ===")
N_chilla, D_chilla = 70e9, 1400e9
print(f"  Params: {N_chilla/1e9:.0f}B  Tokens: {D_chilla/1e9:.0f}B")
print(f"  Compute: {C_chinchilla:.2e} FLOPs (comparable to Gopher)")
print(f"  Loss: {loss(N_chilla, D_chilla):.4f}")
print(f"  Tokens/param: {D_chilla/N_chilla:.1f}")

# The result: Chinchilla achieves lower loss with 4x fewer parameters
# This means 4x cheaper inference at the same quality level.

The planning calculation shows that Gopher was undertrained by a large margin. The 280B parameter model required only 300B tokens by the Kaplan playbook, but the Chinchilla law says the same compute should have been split between a 70B model and 1.4T tokens. The result was a model that beat Gopher on all benchmarks with 4x fewer parameters — making inference dramatically cheaper. This single experiment reshaped how the entire field allocates training compute.

The production constraint that made this actionable: DeepMind had access to a dataset (MassiveText, later filtered versions of C4 and other corpora) with sufficient high-quality tokens. The Chinchilla recipe is only feasible if you have the data. This is why data acquisition, curation, and deduplication have become first-class engineering problems: the scaling law tells you how many tokens you need, and it is often more than organizations initially budgeted.
Common Mistakes
Mistake 1: Over-parameterizing Under a Fixed Compute Budget
The most common mistake after 2020 was continuing to follow the Kaplan scaling recipe and training very large models for relatively few steps. This leaves the model significantly undertrained.

# Wrong: allocate most of the budget to parameters
def wrong_allocation(C_flops):
    # Kaplan-era heuristic: make the model as big as possible
    N = C_flops / (6 * 300e9)  # train for only 300B tokens
    D = 300e9
    return N, D

# Right: use the Chinchilla rule
def right_allocation(C_flops):
    from scaling_laws import optimal_nd
    return optimal_nd(C_flops)

C = 1e23
N_wrong, D_wrong = wrong_allocation(C)
N_right, D_right = right_allocation(C)

print(f"Wrong:  N = {N_wrong/1e9:.0f}B params, D = {D_wrong/1e9:.0f}B tokens, "
      f"tok/param = {D_wrong/N_wrong:.1f}")
print(f"Right:  N = {N_right/1e9:.1f}B params, D = {D_right/1e9:.0f}B tokens, "
      f"tok/param = {D_right/N_right:.1f}")
# The "wrong" model wastes compute on parameters it can't fully utilize.
Mistake 2: Extrapolating Too Far Without Re-fitting

Power law extrapolation is powerful but can fail when you push many orders of magnitude beyond your fitted range. Always gather at least 5–8 training runs covering at least 2 orders of magnitude in scale, and report confidence intervals on your fitted exponents.
Mistake 3: Treating All Tokens as Equivalent

The Chinchilla law was fitted on filtered, deduplicated web text of reasonable quality. If you apply the 20-tokens-per-parameter rule to noisy data (unfiltered Common Crawl, OCR errors, machine-translated text), you will find your actual loss sits above the predicted curve. Data quality multiplies effective token count — a rough rule of thumb is that each pass of deduplication and quality filtering can be worth 2–5x in effective tokens.
Mistake 4: Confusing Loss with Benchmark Performance

Scaling laws predict cross-entropy on the pretraining distribution. Downstream benchmark performance (MMLU, HellaSwag, HumanEval) is correlated but not linearly mapped. A model with 0.05 better pretraining loss might be 5 points better on some benchmarks and indistinguishable on others. Never promise benchmark improvements solely from a scaling law argument.
Best Practices
Run a systematic isoFLOP sweep before committing to a large run:

Train at least 6 models at identical compute budgets (varying N and D), measure final loss, and fit the Chinchilla model. The computational cost of this calibration is typically less than 10% of the final training run but dramatically improves allocation decisions.
Use log-spaced model sizes:

When sampling across model sizes, use logarithmically spaced values (1M, 3M, 10M, 30M, 100M, ...) rather than linearly spaced. Loss as a function of log(N) is approximately linear, so log spacing gives uniform statistical power across the fit.
Always fit and report the irreducible loss separately:
The term
(entropy floor) should be estimated from very large models trained to convergence, not bundled into the fitted coefficients. Getting it wrong shifts all your predictions.
Validate the power law before extrapolating:
Check that your data falls on a straight line in log-log space with
before extrapolating more than 10× in scale. Kinks or curvature indicate that your fitted range does not capture the true underlying law.
Account for architecture-specific compute constants:
The

approximation ignores attention operations. For short sequences and large models, attention FLOPs are negligible; for long context or small models with long sequences, recalculate the constant explicitly.
Track data efficiency separately from compute efficiency:
If you are using data repetition (training on the same tokens more than once), re-run your scaling law calibration. Repeated data reduces the effective token count —
— and shifts the optimal allocation.
Do not reuse coefficients across domains:
Coefficients published for English web text do not apply to code, mathematics, protein sequences, or low-resource languages. Calibrate independently for each domain and report domain-specific curves.
Performance and Scalability Considerations
Compute Cost of the Calibration Sweep
A typical isoFLOP sweep to calibrate a scaling law covers models from 1M to 1B parameters, each trained to completion at the same compute budget. At a pilot compute budget of
FLOPs, this corresponds to training runs ranging from a few GPU-hours to a few GPU-days. The entire calibration sweep for a research team planning a
FLOP production run typically costs less than
FLOPs — about 1% of the final run.
Memory Constraints and the Chinchilla Tradeoff
A subtle production consequence of Chinchilla-optimal training is that compute-optimal models often have fewer parameters than you might expect. While this is great for inference, training a
smaller
model for
more

tokens requires more optimizer steps and more data I/O. For a given hardware configuration, this can hit data pipeline throughput limits before compute limits. Teams have found that data ingestion pipelines need to be tuned carefully when moving from parameter-heavy to token-heavy configurations.
The Irreducible Loss Floor
All scaling laws converge to the irreducible loss
as

. This floor is the entropy of the training data distribution — the information that no model can recover because the signal is genuinely random or missing from the input context. For high-quality curated text, this is estimated around 1.69 nats/token. For noisier data, it is higher. Understanding the irreducible floor is important: once you are within
nats of it, further scaling has diminishing returns and other interventions (better data, architectural improvements, chain-of-thought) become relatively more impactful.
Scaling Beyond the Standard Regime

Multiple research groups have observed that the smooth power law can break down at very large compute budgets, particularly when models are trained with context lengths beyond a few thousand tokens. Long-context training, instruction tuning mid-pretraining, and curriculum learning can all introduce non-monotonicity in the loss curve that standard scaling law models do not capture. Some groups (including DeepMind and Google) have proposed extensions to account for these effects, but no consensus formulation has emerged as of 2025.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Data Collection and Experiment Tracking

# Using Weights & Biases to log scaling law experiments
# pip install wandb torch
import wandb
import math

def run_scaling_experiment(
    n_params: int,
    n_tokens: int,
    model_config: dict,
    run_name: str,
):
    """
    Template for a single isoFLOP sweep experiment.
    Log compute, N, D, and final loss for scaling law analysis.
    """
    C_flops = 6 * n_params * n_tokens

    wandb.init(
        project="scaling-law-sweep",
        name=run_name,
        config={
            "n_params": n_params,
            "n_tokens": n_tokens,
            "compute_flops": C_flops,
            "log_compute": math.log10(C_flops),
            "log_params": math.log10(n_params),
            "tokens_per_param": n_tokens / n_params,
            **model_config,
        }
    )

    # ... your training loop here ...
    # At the end, log the final validation loss:
    # wandb.log({"final_val_loss": val_loss})
    # wandb.finish()


# After running all experiments, download and fit:
def analyze_sweep(project: str = "scaling-law-sweep"):
    api = wandb.Api()
    runs = api.runs(project)

    data = []
    for run in runs:
        if run.state == "finished":
            data.append({
                "N": run.config["n_params"],
                "D": run.config["n_tokens"],
                "loss": run.summary.get("final_val_loss"),
            })
    return data
Key Libraries and Tools
scipy.optimize:
The workhorse for fitting the Chinchilla model via curve_fit or minimize. Available in every scientific Python environment.
Weights & Biases (wandb):
The standard tool for logging and comparing scaling law experiments at the research level. Supports parallel coordinate plots and sweep analysis across hundreds of runs.
Megatron-LM and NanoGPT:
Common training frameworks for running systematic scaling sweeps. NanoGPT is particularly well-suited for small-scale calibration due to its simplicity and speed.
EleutherAI lm-evaluation-harness:
The standard benchmark evaluation suite. Use it to measure downstream task performance alongside cross-entropy loss to understand when scaling law predictions translate to benchmark gains.
Hugging Face Transformers:
Provides standardized model configurations and training loops that make it straightforward to sweep over model sizes while keeping architecture constant.

The Chinchilla formulation is the current consensus starting point for practical compute planning. It is not a complete theory: it says nothing about emergent capabilities, data quality corrections, or non-standard training regimes (RLHF, distillation, multi-task learning). For these, practitioners combine Chinchilla as a baseline with task-specific empirical sweeps.
Security and Reliability Considerations
Training Instability at Large Scale

Scaling laws assume that training converges normally. In practice, frontier-scale training runs encounter loss spikes, gradient explosions, and sudden divergence that cannot be predicted from small-scale experiments. A scaling law prediction of a target loss is conditional on a stable run — instability events can consume significant compute without contributing to the fitted relationship. Production training systems use gradient clipping, careful learning rate warmup and decay, and checkpoint rollback strategies to mitigate these risks.
Overfitting the Power Law

A subtle reliability risk: if you fit your scaling law on too few data points, or on runs that share systematic biases (same dataset, same random seed, same hardware), the fitted exponents will not generalize. Always use at least 5–8 diverse training runs for fitting, and validate the fit on held-out runs before extrapolating. A fitted
on the log-log linearization is a warning sign that your data does not support a clean power law in the observed range.
Data Contamination and Distribution Shift

Scaling laws are fitted and validated assuming the training data distribution is fixed. In practice, repeated data passes, web crawl snapshots from different years, and inadvertent benchmark contamination all shift the effective distribution. If your validation set overlaps with your training data (even partially), your fitted loss values will be optimistically biased, and the extrapolated predictions will overestimate actual model quality on clean held-out distributions.
Governance and Responsible Scaling

Scaling laws have policy implications. Organizations including Anthropic and OpenAI have published Responsible Scaling Policies (RSPs) that use scaling law extrapolations to forecast when future models might cross capability thresholds that require additional safety measures. The reliability of these forecasts depends directly on the quality of the underlying scaling law calibration. Incorrect exponent estimates in either direction carry real risks: underestimating how fast capability scales can mean insufficient safety preparation; overestimating can misdirect safety resources. Calibration on multiple independent pilot runs and uncertainty quantification are therefore not merely engineering practices but policy-relevant responsibilities.
Key Takeaways
Scaling laws are power laws:
Loss decreases as
where X is parameters, data, or compute — making improvement predictable and smooth across many orders of magnitude.
The Chinchilla law overturned the Kaplan prescription:
The original scaling law said to scale parameters much faster than data; the 2022 Chinchilla paper showed that both should scale equally, with roughly 20 tokens per parameter at the optimal point.
GPT-3 was undertrained:
At 175B parameters and 300B tokens, GPT-3 was far from compute-optimal. An equivalent compute budget would have been better spent on a 7–10B parameter model trained on 1.7T+ tokens.
The compute formula is C ≈ 6ND:
For dense transformer training, FLOPs consumed equals approximately 6 times parameters times tokens. This constraint, combined with the loss model, fully determines the optimal allocation.
IsoFLOP sweeps are the practical tool:

To calibrate scaling laws for your specific architecture and data, run multiple training experiments at the same compute budget but different (N, D) splits, then fit the Chinchilla model to observed losses.
Scaling laws do not predict emergent capabilities:
Cross-entropy loss and benchmark performance have a complex relationship. Capabilities like multi-step reasoning appear discontinuously and cannot be extrapolated from a smooth power law.
Data quality acts as an effective multiplier on token count:
The 20-tokens-per-parameter rule assumes high-quality, deduplicated training data. Noisy data requires more tokens to achieve the same effective training signal.
Responsible scaling requires calibrated uncertainty:
Organizations use scaling law extrapolations for safety planning. Exponent uncertainty must be quantified and reported; point estimates alone are insufficient for policy decisions.
The field is still evolving:
Post-Chinchilla refinements (accounting for data repetition, long-context training, mixture-of-experts, and multi-modal data) are active research areas with no settled consensus as of mid-2026.
